title: Reading Stack Traces
intro: Your app just crashed and the console is showing a scary wall of text called a 'stack trace.' Imagine it like a trail of breadcrumbs through a maze -- each line shows you one step closer to where things went wrong. Don't panic. Let's learn to read it.

teach.title: A Stack Trace Is Like a Trail of Breadcrumbs
teach.body: Imagine you walk through five rooms and trip in the last one. A stack trace is like a list of every room you walked through, in order, with the room you tripped in listed first. It shows the chain of function calls that led to the crash.

teach.title: A Stack Trace Is Like a Call History on Your Phone
teach.body: When you miss a call and want to know who called, you check your call history -- it shows the most recent call at the top. A stack trace is your code's call history. The most recent function call (where the crash happened) is at the top, and each line below shows who called whom, leading all the way back to where things started.

teach.title: How to Read a Stack Trace
teach.body:
A stack trace reads **top to bottom**:

1. The **top line** is where the error actually happened -- start here
2. Each line below it shows which function called the one above it
3. **Skip framework lines** (like react-dom or node internals) -- focus on YOUR files

The top line of your own code is almost always where the bug is.

teach.title: A Real Stack Trace
teach.code:
TypeError: Cannot read properties of null
    at UserCard (src/UserCard.jsx:8)      <-- YOUR code, start here
    at renderWithHooks (react-dom.js:14985)  <-- framework, skip
    at App (src/App.jsx:22)               <-- YOUR code, called UserCard
teach.annotation: Start at the top line that mentions YOUR files (UserCard.jsx:8). That's where the crash happened. App.jsx:22 is where UserCard was called from.

teach.body: Stack traces look intimidating, but reading them is a superpower. Vibecoding pros who can decode a stack trace fix crashes in minutes instead of hours -- and that efficiency is what makes building and selling apps profitable.
Read stack traces from the top. The first line with your own file name is where the bug is. Skip lines from frameworks and libraries -- the problem is almost never in their code.

start.narrative:
Your React app crashes with this error:

TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'map')
    at ProductList (src/components/ProductList.jsx:12)
    at renderWithHooks (react-dom.development.js:14985)
    at mountIndeterminateComponent (react-dom.development.js:17811)
    at App (src/App.jsx:8)

Where do you look first?

choices.text: The top of the stack trace: ProductList.jsx line 12
The bottom of the stack trace: App.jsx line 8
The react-dom lines -- the error is probably in React itself
The 'products' variable is null when this line runs
The .map() function is broken
The <li> element is causing the crash

wrong-bottom.narrative: Stack traces read top-to-bottom, where the top is the most recent call -- the actual crash site. The bottom is where the call chain started. Start at the top and look for YOUR files.

choices.text: OK, look at the top: ProductList.jsx line 12

wrong-framework.narrative: react-dom and node_modules lines almost never contain your bug. They appear because your code called into the framework. Skip framework lines and look for YOUR source files.

choices.text: OK, focus on my own files in the trace

wrong-map.narrative: The .map() function works fine -- it's a built-in array method. The problem is what you're calling .map() ON. The error says you're calling .map() on null.

choices.text: So 'products' is null when this line runs

wrong-li.narrative: The JSX inside .map() never runs -- the crash happens before the callback executes. The error is that 'null' has no property 'map'. What you're calling .map() on is null.

choices.text: So the products variable is null
The render runs once with products=null on initial mount, before the fetch resolves
useQuery is buggy and returns null randomly
fetchProducts must be returning null from the API

choose-fix.narrative: Initial mount with no data -- a normal async lifecycle. Now choose the fix. The user-facing behavior matters here, not just the absence of a crash.

choices.text:
Render

Loading...

while products is null, then the list when data arrives
Default to an empty array: (products || []).map(...)
Wrap the .map in a try/catch and ignore the error silently

regression-test.narrative: The crash is fixed locally. But unless something stops the next contributor from removing your guard, the bug returns. What test pins this behavior?

choices.text: Render ProductList with products=null and assert the loading state appears (no crash)
Render ProductList with products=[{name:'a'}] and assert the item renders
Add a console.log so you'd notice if it crashes again

ship-safely.narrative: Fix in hand, regression test in CI. Last decision: how do you ship? The crash is happening for users right now.

choices.text: Hotfix to production immediately, monitor error rates for 30 minutes
Merge to main and wait for the next scheduled release tomorrow
Sit on it -- the regression test is enough proof

end-good.narrative: You decoded the trace top-down, traced products to a useQuery race, picked the user-friendly loading-state fix, locked it with a null-render regression test, and hotfixed production while watching error metrics. The crash count drops to zero in minutes and a future contributor cannot accidentally undo your fix.
end-good.feedback: Full debugging loop: read the trace, find the source, choose the right user-facing fix, prevent recurrence with a test, and ship safely. The pattern of 'check for null before using async data' is something you'll do every day in real React apps.

end-okay.narrative: The fix is in main with a regression test. But users keep crashing for another 24 hours while you wait for the release window. The pipeline you built was correct; the urgency wasn't.
end-okay.feedback: You did the engineering right. Production crashes deserve hotfixes -- the same regression test runs whether you ship now or tomorrow. Match the deploy speed to the user impact.

end-poor.narrative: Code is fixed locally, test exists, but nothing ships. Users keep hitting the same crash. The regression test will catch the issue if anyone touches the file again -- but that's no help to the user staring at a blank screen right now.
end-poor.feedback: Tests prevent the bug from coming back; deploys remove the bug that's already there. You need both. Ship the fix.
